item.title: apple smartphone desk laptop 1282241
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Image by Pexels from Pixabay

item.title: How to Implement Message-Driven Services with Spring Cloud Stream
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

In our previous articles, we have discussed the REST standard primarily when discussing micro-service communication styles. Message-driven communication is an alternative that guarantees asynchronous, highly decoupled interaction. This article will teach you how to implement message-driven services using Spring Cloud.

Message-Driven Services

We can distinguish two main different patterns based on messages:

  • Point-to-point
  • Publish-subscribe

Point-to-point involves exchanging messages between two established endpoints. In the public-subscribe model, on the other hand, messages are sent to a so-called topic. Every service that wants to consume those messages must register as a listener to a topic. This article will focus on the publish-subscribe model, i.e. the best way to reduce coupling between components.

The basic components of messaging-based systems are producers and consumers. A producer sends messages created by some source to a remote destination (a topic), and a consumer registers to the same destination to receive the messages.

The exchange of messages is done through specialized software components called message brokers. Examples are Rabbit MQ and Kafka. We can integrate micro-services based on Spring Cloud Stream and message brokers through the so-called binders. A binder provides a broker-specific implementation of the Spring Cloud Stream abstraction.

Once we configure a specific binder, we have established an integration between our Spring Cloud Stream application and a broker. Using Spring Cloud Stream and broker-specific configurations, we can define the basic connection properties and the bindings between our application and broker topics, that we can call destinations.

Message-Driven Style Broker Running and Configuration

To have a functioning sample we must have a running message broker. If we use RabbitMQ, we can run it by using docker, with a docker-compose file like this:

version: '3'
services:
  rabbitmq:
    image: rabbitmq:management
    ports:
      - 5672:5672
      - 15672:15672

To start it we have to position ourselves in the folder where the docker-compose file is and enter the command "./docker compose up" in the command line. Once we have RabbitMS running, we can configure it in our application.xml file:

spring:
   application:
      name: booksmanagement-service
   rabbitmq:
      host: localhost
      port: 5672

Message-Driven Style Implemented by Java Functional Interfaces

In the recent versions of Spring Cloud Stream, message-driven services can use Java functional interfaces to implicitly implement message producers and consumers. Three interfaces are used:

  • Consumer<T>, where T is the type of input
  • Supplier<T>, where T is the type of input
  • Function<T, R>, where T is the type of input and R is the type of the result

The Consumer interface, as its name implies, is implemented to provide a consumer in the publish-subscribe pattern, while a Supplier object implements a producer. The Function interface is a mixing of the two, it implements both a producer and a consumer in a single object. It connects to the broker to send a message to a topic and at the same time returns a value to an output topic.

To provide implementations of those three interfaces to obtain implicit producers and consumers we define Spring beans in the application, as in the following example:

@SpringBootApplication
public class AppMain {

	public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SpringApplicationBuilder(AppMain.class).run(args);
	}

    @Bean
    public Consumer<String> sink() {
        return payload -> {
            System.out.println(payload);
        };
    }

    @Bean
    public Supplier<String> source() {
        return () -> {
            String message = "Supplier message";
            System.out.println("Sending value: " + message);
            return message;

        };
    }

    @Bean
    public Function<String, String> uppercase() {
        return payload -> {
            System.out.println(payload);
            return payload.toUpperCase();
        };
    }
}

The name of the bean translates automatically to a binding with the format <function-name>-in-<index> for consumers and <function-name>-out-<index> for producers. The index part is 0 by default and has a particular significance only in the case of functions with multiple input/output arguments.

We can map the function names to remote destinations (topics or queues), like in the following piece of configuration: 

spring:
   application:
      name: booksmanagement-service
   rabbitmq:
      host: localhost
      port: 5672 
   cloud:
      function:
         definition: uppercase;receive;send;source
      stream:
         bindings:
            uppercase-in-0:
               destination: test-out    
               error-handler-definition: errorHandler           
            uppercase-out-0:
               destination: test-in
            sink-in-0:
               destination: test-in
            source-out-0:
               destination: test-out

Error Handling

We can attach an error handler to a function by defining a specific Consumer bean with ErrorMessage as input type:

    @Bean
    public Consumer<ErrorMessage> errorHandler() {
        return error -> {
            System.out.println(error.getPayload());
            System.out.println(error.getOriginalMessage());
        };
    }

The error handler can then be associated with the function like in the following example:

      stream:
         bindings:
            uppercase-in-0:
               destination: test-out    
               error-handler-definition: errorHandler

Message-Driven Services Triggered by REST Calls

If we need to produce messages by some external REST call, we have to use a StreamBridge service. We can inject this service into a controller and use its send method, passing to it a binding and a payload:

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/booksmanagement")
public class BooksManagementController {

    @Autowired
    private StreamBridge streamBridge;

    @GetMapping(value = "/sendBookInfo")
    public void sendBookInfo() {
        BookInfo bookInfo = new BookInfo();
        bookInfo.setId(1L);
        Book book = new Book();
        book.setTitle("The Naked Sun");
        book.setGenre("Sci-fi");
        Author author = new Author();
        author.setName("Isaac Asimov");
        author.setBiographyInfo("Biography Info");

        System.out.println("Sending " + bookInfo);
        streamBridge.send("sendBookInfo-out-0", bookInfo);
    }

In the application.xml we then define a function with the same name as the controller method and associate it with the same binding we passed as input to the send method:

   cloud:
      function:
         definition: sendBookInfo
      stream:
         bindings:
            sendBookInfo-out-0:
               destination: bookInfo-out

Grouping

In micro-service applications, we normally run multiple instances for each service. If the micro-service acts as a consumer for a specific topic, we normally do not want all the instances consuming the same message. We can avoid this by using the grouping functionality. Defining a group for a binding definition allows only one instance of the group at a time to consume a specific message.

For instance, we can implement a sample by sending messages to a topic using a StreamBridge service:

    @GetMapping(value = "/sendBookInfoGrouped")
    public void sendBookInfoGrouped() {
        BookInfo bookInfo = new BookInfo();
        bookInfo.setId(1L);
        Book book = new Book();
        book.setTitle("The Naked Sun");
        book.setGenre("Sci-fi");
        Author author = new Author();
        author.setName("Isaac Asimov");
        author.setBiographyInfo("Biography Info");

        System.out.println("Sending " + bookInfo);
        streamBridge.send("sendBookInfoGrouped-out-0", bookInfo);
    }

We define the binding in the configuration file of the producer side:

   cloud:
      function:
         definition: sendBookInfoGrouped
      stream:
         bindings:
            sendBookInfoGrouped-out-0:
               destination: bookInfoGrouped-out

And in the consumer part, we define a group for the binding definition:

   cloud:
      function:
         definition: receiveBookInfoGroup
      stream:
         bindings:
            receiveBookInfoGroup-in-0:
               destination: bookInfoGrouped-out
               group: bookInfoGroupBooks

Partitioning

A typical requirement in message-driven architectures is to partition the input data so single service instances elaborate single partitions. To partition the data we have to use some attribute of the payload. In the example below we add the id attribute of a BookInfo object as a property of the header, named bookInfoId.

    @GetMapping(value = "/sendBookInfoPartitioned")
    public void sendBookInfoPartitioned() {
        BookInfo bookInfo = new BookInfo();
        bookInfo.setId(1L);
        Book book = new Book();
        book.setTitle("The Naked Sun");
        book.setGenre("Sci-fi");
        Author author = new Author();
        author.setName("Isaac Asimov");
        author.setBiographyInfo("Biography Info");

        System.out.println("Sending " + bookInfo);
        streamBridge.send("sendBookInfoPartitioned-out-0", MessageBuilder.withPayload(bookInfo)
            .setHeader("bookInfoId", bookInfo.getId())
            .build());
    }

In the binding configuration, we need two additional properties:

  • partitionKeyExpression: headers['bookInfoId']
  • partitionCount: 4

As in the following application.xml fragment:

   cloud:
      function:
         definition: sendBookInfoPartitioned
      stream:
         bindings:
            sendBookInfoPartitioned-out-0:
               destination: bookInfoPartitioned-out
               producer:
                  partitionKeyExpression: headers['bookInfoId']
                  partitionCount: 4

The partitionKeyExpression property defines in general an expression that serves the purpose of partitioning the data. It could be any expression, and in this case, it is the bookInfoId attribute retrieved from the header. The partitionCount property defines how many partitions are used. The consumer part will use this number to decide which instance will consume a particular partition.

By default, Spring Cloud Stream will calculate the hash of the partitionKeyExpression property and the module of it and partitionCount: key.hash()%partitionCount. In our example, this will be a number from 0 to 3, and on the consumer side we can set which partition each instance will elaborate by a binding configuration like this:

   cloud:
      function:
         definition: receiveBookInfoPartitioned
      stream:
         bindings:
            receiveBookInfoPartitioned-in-0:
               destination: bookInfoPartitioned-out
               group: bookInfoGroupBooks
               consumer:
                  partitioned: true
                  instance-index: 0
                  instance-count: 4

We define the consumer as partitioned setting the partitioned parameter to true, and assign a number from 0 to 3 to the instance-index parameter. The instance-count parameter defines the number of instances working on the partitioned data.

Conclusion

The message-driven style of communication has particular importance in micro-service systems. It allows for asynchronous interaction and loose coupling of the services. You can find the source code of the example of this article on GitHub.
item.excerpt:encoded: In this article, you will learn how to implement message-driven micro-service systems with Spring Cloud Stream.

item.title: How to Implement Observability with Spring Cloud
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Observability is a concept involved in assessing a running system. It gives the means to measure the relevant metrics and evaluate how the software works internally. Spring Boot provides Micrometer and Micrometer Tracing packages to support metrics, logging, and tracing. External software provides an overall set of tools to deal with observability. This article will discuss a stack based on Spring Boot 3, Micrometer, Micrometer Tracing, Grafana, Prometheus, Tempo, and Loki.

Observability with Micrometer

In a Spring Boot application, we can enforce observability by the Micrometer library. To define an observation we must use an ObservationRegistry and register an ObservationHandler in it. An ObservationHandler defines a set of specific methods that are called to start and close observations, observation events, and scopes:

  • onStart: an observation starts
  • onStop: an observation ends
  • onEvent: an event that happens during an observation
  • onScopeOpened: an observation scope starts. Thread local objects can be passed in and closed when the scope ends
  • onScopeClosed: an observation scope closes
  • onError: an error occurs during an observation

These methods are called passing in an observation context as an argument.

Observability Server-Side Implementation

To illustrate how observability works we can start with an example. We implement two simple Spring Boot applications: a Book client exposing a getAuthor REST service that calls a remote Author service to get an author information by its name.

The Author's application will expose a service like this:

    @GetMapping("/author/{name}")
    public Optional<Author> findByName(@PathVariable("name") String name) {
       ...
    }

The Book service will have a getAuthor endpoint with the following implementation:

    @GetMapping(value = "/getAuthor", params = { "authorName" })
    public Optional<Author> getAuthor(@RequestParam("authorName") String authorName) {
       ...
    }

Configuring the Author's server application

The Author service plays the role of the server. To implement observability we have to provide several dependencies. To handle the Micrometer metrics we use Prometheus with the following dependency:

		<!-- Prometheus -->
		<dependency>
			<groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
			<artifactId>micrometer-registry-prometheus</artifactId>
		</dependency>

To support Micrometer Tracing in the correlation of requests into single spans we add Zipking Brave:

		<!-- Zipkin Brave -->
		<dependency>
			<groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
			<artifactId>micrometer-tracing-bridge-brave</artifactId>
		</dependency>

We must provide spans to a server with a specific format to obtain latency visualization. To do this we need a zipkin-reporter-brave library:

		<!-- Zipkin Latency Visualization -->
		<dependency>
			<groupId>io.zipkin.reporter2</groupId>
			<artifactId>zipkin-reporter-brave</artifactId>
		</dependency>

To send the logs, which are correlated by a tracer ID, to Graphana, we need the loki-logback-appender package:

		<!-- Grafana Loki Appender-->
		<dependency>
			<groupId>com.github.loki4j</groupId>
			<artifactId>loki-logback-appender</artifactId>
			<version>1.4.2</version>
		</dependency>

To make Micrometer support OpenFeign we add also the following:

		<!-- Openfeign Micrometer Support -->
		<dependency>
		    <groupId>io.github.openfeign</groupId>
		     <artifactId>feign-micrometer</artifactId>
		</dependency>

We then need some configuration in the application.xml file. We set the actuator and metrics to include a Prometheus endpoint, the sampling probability property to 1.0 to use all traces for latency analysis, and enable percentiles histograms:

management:
   tracing:
      sampling:
         probability: 1.0
   endpoints:
      web:
         exposure:
            include: prometheus

   metrics:
      distribution:
         percentiles-histogram:
            http:
               server:
                  requests: 'true'
              
logging:
  pattern:
    level: '%5p [${spring.application.name:},%X{traceId:-},%X{spanId:-}]'

We also provide a configuration for Logback to send logs to Loki:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>
    <include resource="org/springframework/boot/logging/logback/base.xml" />
    <springProperty scope="context" name="appName" source="spring.application.name"/>

    <appender name="LOKI" class="com.github.loki4j.logback.Loki4jAppender">
        <http>
            <url>http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/push</url>
        </http>
        <format>
            <label>
                <pattern>app=${appName},host=${HOSTNAME},traceID=%X{traceId:-NONE},level=%level</pattern>
            </label>
            <message>
                <pattern>${FILE_LOG_PATTERN}</pattern>
            </message>
            <sortByTime>true</sortByTime>
        </format>
    </appender>

    <root level="INFO">
        <appender-ref ref="LOKI"/>
    </root>
</configuration>

Configuring the Book's client application

The configuration for the client side is almost the same, the only difference is the lack of a histogram configuration fragment:

management:
   tracing:
      sampling:
         probability: 1.0
   endpoints:
      web:
         exposure:
            include: prometheus
        
logging:
  pattern:
    level: '%5p [${spring.application.name:},%X{traceId:-},%X{spanId:-}]'

Server Application Code

On the server side, we first define an ObservationHandler to capture the onStart and onStop events of the observation:

@Component
public class ObsHandler implements ObservationHandler<Observation.Context> {

    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(ObsHandler.class);

    private String getAuthorTypeFromContext(Observation.Context context) {
        return StreamSupport.stream(context.getLowCardinalityKeyValues().spliterator(), false)
            .filter(keyValue -> "authorType".equals(keyValue.getKey()))
                .map(KeyValue::getValue)
                .findFirst()
                .orElse("UNKNOWN");
    }

    @Override
    public void onStart(Observation.Context context) {
        log.info("Before running the observation for context [{}], userType [{}]", context.getName(), getAuthorTypeFromContext(context));
    }

    @Override
    public void onStop(Observation.Context context) {
        log.info("After running the observation for context [{}], userType [{}]", context.getName(), getAuthorTypeFromContext(context));
    }

    @Override
    public boolean supportsContext(Observation.Context context) {
        return true;
    }
}

We also configure an ObservedAspect bean to use the @Observed annotation:

@Configuration(proxyBeanMethods = false)
class ObsConfiguration {

    @Bean
    ObservedAspect observedAspect(ObservationRegistry observationRegistry) {
        return new ObservedAspect(observationRegistry);
    }
}

We finally annotate the method that the client application will cal with @Observed. This way, we are creating an observation on that method:

    @Observed(name = "authorInfo", contextualName = "getting-author-info", lowCardinalityKeyValues = { "authorType", "Poet" })
    public Optional<Author> findByName(String name) {
        return authorRepository.findById(name);
    }

Client Application Code

On the client side, we define an ObservationHandler as in the server side and we manually create an observation in the method that calls the server side, with a low cardinality key named "authorType" and high cardinality key named "authorName":

    @GetMapping(value = "/getAuthor", params = { "authorName" })
    public Optional<Author> getAuthor(@RequestParam("authorName") String authorName) {
        ObservationRegistry registry = ObservationRegistry.create();
        registry.observationConfig()
            .observationHandler(new ObsHandler());
        ThreadLocal<Optional<Author>> thLocal = new ThreadLocal<Optional<Author>>();
        Observation.createNotStarted("my.observation", registry)
            .lowCardinalityKeyValue("authorType", "Poet")
            .highCardinalityKeyValue("authorName", authorName)
            .contextualName("getAuthorInfo")
            .observe(() -> {
                logger.info("request to the server");
                Optional<Author> response = bookService.getAuthor(authorName);
                thLocal.set(response);
                logger.info("response [{}]", response);
            });

        return thLocal.get();
    }

Running the Sample

You can run all the components required to observe our couple of demo applications using a docker-compose yaml file. You have to save the file in a directory of your choice and execute "docker compose up" on the command line. You can access Grafana and go to http://localhost:3000 and Prometheus at http://localhost:9000.

You then run the client and server applications and you are all set. On Graphana you can open the dashboard related to logs, traces, and metrics. There you can pick a traceid, and see all logs and traces related to it. You will also see the latency metrics provided by default by Spring Boot with the support of Micrometer.

You can also see the metrics related to the method annotated with @Observed on the server side and the manually created observation on the client side by opening the Prometheus view.

Conclusion

Assessing a system once in production is crucial. Spring provides solid support to this with its Micrometer and Micrometer Tracing features. You can find the source code of the example of this article on GitHub.
item.excerpt:encoded: In this article, you will learn how to implement observabilty for micro-service systems with Micrometer and Micrometer Tracing.

item.title: magnifier newspaper history glass 424566
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Image by shotput from Pixabay

item.title: How to Implement Contract Tests with Pact
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

When we develop a micro-service system with many REST APIs, we face the problem of testing it. In addition to unit, integration, and end-to-end tests, we must encompass contract testing. Contract testing involves verifying how the interfaces provided by a service satisfy the provider's requirements. In this article, we will see how to implement contract testing with the Pact software.

A Few Words on Pact

Pact is a tool for performing contract-based testing. It is consumer-driven, which means that it bases the "contract" on the consumer interface. Once the contract is written and stored in a shared place, the producer and consumer can test it independently.

We can use two Junit5-based packages that implement Pact for consumers and producers. A maven plugin is also available to generate the contract in the consumer part. To store the contract we can use a Pact broker. In this case, we can configure the plugin with the host and port of the broker.

Pact Configuration on the Consumer

Suppose we have a Book service with a getAuthor method, that calls an Author service to retrieve some information about an author by its name:

    @GetMapping(value = "/findAuthor", params = { "name" })
    public Optional<Author> getAuthor(@RequestParam("name") String name) {
        return bookService.getAuthor(name);
    }

To support Pact we need the following Maven dependencies:

		<dependency>
		    <groupId>au.com.dius.pact.consumer</groupId>
		    <artifactId>junit5</artifactId>
		    <version>4.6.10</version>
		    <scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>
		<dependency>
		    <groupId>au.com.dius.pact.provider</groupId>
		    <artifactId>junit5spring</artifactId>
		    <version>4.6.10</version>
		    <scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>

We also add the following, to support Assertj assertions:

		<dependency>
		    <groupId>org.assertj</groupId>
		    <artifactId>assertj-core</artifactId>
		    <scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>

We also add a Maven plugin. It generates the contract in JSON format and stores it in a Pact broker:

            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
                <configuration>
                    <systemPropertyVariables>
                        <pact.rootDir>target/pacts</pact.rootDir>
                    </systemPropertyVariables>
                </configuration>
            </plugin>
			<plugin>
			 <groupId>au.com.dius</groupId>
			 <artifactId>pact-jvm-provider-maven_2.11</artifactId>
			 <version>3.5.24</version>
			 <configuration>
			   <pactDirectory>target/pacts</pactDirectory>
			   <pactBrokerUrl>http://localhost:9292</pactBrokerUrl>
			   <trimSnapshot>true</trimSnapshot>
			</configuration>
			</plugin>

We can launch the Pact broker by Docker, using a docker-compose file like the following:

version: '3'
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:13
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: pactbroker
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
      POSTGRES_DB: pactbroker
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  pactbroker:
    image: pactfoundation/pact-broker
    depends_on:
      - postgres
    environment:
      PACT_BROKER_DATABASE_ADAPTER: postgres
      PACT_BROKER_DATABASE_USERNAME: pactbroker
      PACT_BROKER_DATABASE_PASSWORD: password
      PACT_BROKER_DATABASE_HOST: postgres
      PACT_BROKER_DATABASE_NAME: pactbroker
      PACT_BROKER_PORT: 9292
    ports:
      - "9292:9292"

volumes:
  postgres_data:

As we can see from the above, the Pact broker uses a Postgres database behind the scenes.

Test implementation

We implement the test for the consumer part by providing the PactConsumerTestExt class to the @ExtendWith annotation. We define a createPact method to create and return the contract. In this case, we define how the findAuthor service behaves. Then we run a test on our contract definition by using the @PactTestFor(pactMethod = "createPact") annotation:

@ExtendWith(PactConsumerTestExt.class)
public class PactConsumerDrivenContractUnitTest {

    @Pact(consumer = "ConsumerService", provider = "ProviderService")
    public V4Pact createPact(PactDslWithProvider builder) {
        Map<String, String> headers = new HashMap<>();
        headers.put("Content-Type", "application/json");

        return builder
          .given("test GET")
            .uponReceiving("GET REQUEST")
            .path("/authors/findAuthor")
            .query("name=Goethe")
            .method("GET")
          .willRespondWith()
            .status(200)
            .headers(headers)
            .body("{\"name\":\"Goethe\",\"biography\":\"Bio info\"}")
            .toPact(V4Pact.class);
    }

    @Test
    @PactTestFor(pactMethod = "createPact")
    void runTest(MockServer mockServer) {
        RestTemplate restTemplate = new RestTemplate();
        String response = restTemplate.getForObject(mockServer.getUrl() + "/authors/findAuthor?name=Goethe", String.class);
        assertEquals("{\"name\":\"Goethe\",\"biography\":\"Bio info\"}", response);

    }
}

Pact Configuration on the Producer

On the producer side, we set the same dependencies in the pom.xml, but we don't need the maven plugin to generate and store the contract as on the consumer side. We define a test providing the Pact broker URL and marking the class with @Provider("ProviderService"):

@Provider("ProviderService")
@ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class)
@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
@PactBroker(url = "http://localhost:9292")
@AutoConfigureMockMvc
public class ProviderPactTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @TestTemplate
    @ExtendWith(PactVerificationInvocationContextProvider.class)
    void pactVerificationTestTemplate(PactVerificationContext context) {
        context.verifyInteraction();
    }

    @BeforeEach
    void setup(PactVerificationContext context) {
        MockMvcTestTarget testTarget = new MockMvcTestTarget(mockMvc);
        context.setTarget(testTarget);
    }

    @State(value = "test GET", action = StateChangeAction.SETUP)
    void testGetExists() {
    }

}

Conclusion

Pact is a very useful tool to implement contract testing. An implementation of Pact features is available in Spring for the consumer and provider side. You can find the source code of the example of this article on GitHub.
item.excerpt:encoded: In this article, you will learn how to implement contract testing for micro-service systems with Spring-enabled Pact libraries.

item.title: penmanship pen signature document 2561217
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded: Image by StockSnap from Pixabay

item.title: Spring Cloud Contract: How to Implement Contract Tests
item.dc:creator: mario.casari@gmail.com
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

We have already seen how to use Pact to perform contract testing in a previous article. Spring Cloud provides its solution with Spring Cloud Contract. In this article, we will see how to implement a test using this tool for a couple of simple REST-based micro-services.

A Few Words on Spring Cloud Contract

Spring Cloud Contract supports the implementation of contract tests for JVM-based applications. It includes three main components:

  • Spring Cloud Verifier
  • Spring Cloud Stub Runner
  • Spring Cloud WireMock

It defines the contracts using a Groovy DSL syntax. These definition files generate JSON stubs that are then included in a jar and deployed in a Maven repository. Differently from Pact, the contract creation is started on the producer side.

Spring Cloud Contract Configuration on the Producer

. The first thing to do in the producer application is to set the spring-cloud-starter-contract-verifier dependency:

		<dependency>
		    <groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
		    <artifactId>spring-cloud-starter-contract-verifier</artifactId>
		    <scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>

We also need a plugin to generate the stubs from the definitions and tests extending a base class:

			<plugin>
			    <groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
			    <artifactId>spring-cloud-contract-maven-plugin</artifactId>
			    <version>4.1.3</version>
			    <extensions>true</extensions>
			    <configuration>
			        <baseClassForTests>
			            com.codingstrain.springcloud.sample.libraryapp.authors.BaseTestClass
			        </baseClassForTests>
			    </configuration>
			</plugin>

In the /test/resources folder of our project, we include a Groovy definition file we call testProducer.groovy in our example. It will contain the following:

import org.springframework.cloud.contract.spec.Contract

Contract.make {
    description "return biography info for an author named Goethe"
    request {
        method GET()
        url("/authors/findAuthor") {
            queryParameters {
                parameter("name", "Goethe")
            }
        }
    }
    response {
        body("{\"name\":\"Goethe\",\"biography\":\"Bio info\"}")
        status 200
    }
}

The base class will be:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.MOCK)
@DirtiesContext
@AutoConfigureMessageVerifier
public class BaseTestClass {

    @Autowired
    private AuthorController authorController;

    @BeforeEach
    public void setup() {
        StandaloneMockMvcBuilder standaloneMockMvcBuilder 
            = MockMvcBuilders.standaloneSetup(authorController);
        RestAssuredMockMvc.standaloneSetup(standaloneMockMvcBuilder);
    }
}

If we run Maven install a jar will be generated and installed in the local Maven repository with the JSON stub. Furthermore, a test will be generated that extends the base class and follows the Groovy definition file configuration.

Spring Cloud Contract Configuration on the Consumer

On the consumer side, we set the following dependencies:

		<dependency>
			<groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
			<artifactId>spring-cloud-contract-wiremock</artifactId>
			<scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>
		<dependency>
			<groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
			<artifactId>spring-cloud-contract-stub-runner</artifactId>
			<scope>test</scope>
		</dependency>

We then create a test class like this:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.MOCK)
@AutoConfigureMockMvc
@AutoConfigureJsonTesters
@AutoConfigureStubRunner(
  stubsMode = StubRunnerProperties.StubsMode.LOCAL,
    ids = "springcloud.sample:springcloud.sample.libraryapp.testing.contract.authors:+:stubs:8091")
public class ConsumerTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @Test
    public void testConsumer()
      throws Exception {
 
        mockMvc.perform(MockMvcRequestBuilders.get("/library/findAuthor?name=Goethe")
          .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON))
          .andExpect(status().isOk())
            .andExpect(content().string("{\"name\":\"Goethe\",\"biography\":\"Bio info\"}"));
    }
}

The test above uses the stub in the local maven repository to perform the test.

Conclusion

Spring Cloud Contract is a valuable alternative to Pact. We have seen how a contract stub can be generated as a jar and installed in the local Maven repository and how tests can be performed on the producer and consumer side. You can find the source code of the example of this article on GitHub.
item.excerpt:encoded: In this article, you will learn how to implement contract testing for micro-service systems with Spring Cloud Contract.
